blocks.content: Hester's had been on the corner of Fifth and Birch since before any of them were born, and it smelled like it — like decades of fried things and sweet tea and the particular brand of vanilla milkshake that no other place in Millhaven had ever successfully replicated. The booths were red vinyl, cracked at the corners, and the menus were laminated and sticky and never changed because nothing at Hester's ever changed, which was most of the point.

Eli had already claimed the big corner booth when they got there, which meant he'd been there long enough to get a Coke, which meant he'd been there at least fifteen minutes before noon-thirty, which nobody commented on because it was Eli and this was what Eli did when he was excited about something.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: I already know what everyone's getting. Kit gets the double with no onion. Daisy gets the regular with extra pickles and a chocolate shake. Savannah gets the chicken and acts like she's not jealous of our burgers.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Nobody genuinely prefers the chicken at Hester's.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: You look at my burger every single time.

blocks.speaker: Savannah
blocks.content: I look at everything. I'm observant.
Kit slid in across from Daisy — the booth arranged itself this way without anyone deciding it, had been arranged this way for two years — and picked up the sticky menu he didn't need.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You don't understand a lot of things.

The waitress came. Her name was Gloria and she had worked at Hester's forever and always called them hon without distinction, which made Eli feel seen in a way that nothing else in his life currently did.

blocks.speaker: Gloria
blocks.content: Last Saturday of summer, hons. What'll it be?
Eli ordered for the whole table without being asked. Gloria didn't write any of it down.
